[[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ]]{{Gion cult}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[en:Yasaka Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]] {{Commons category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[de:Yasaka-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]] [[es:Santuario Yasaka]] [[fa:معبد یاساکا]] [[fr:Yasaka-jinja]] [[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]] [[id:Kuil Yasaka]] [[it:Santuario di Yasaka]] [[ja:八坂神社]] [[ko:야사카 신사]] [[pt:Santuário Yasaka]] [[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]] [[simple:Yasaka Shrine]] [[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]] [[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]] [[uk:Храм Ясака]] [[zh:八坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q692714}} [[Category:656 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 650s]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has English label]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]]

[[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ]]{{Gion cult}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[en:Yasaka Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]] {{Commons category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[de:Yasaka-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]] [[es:Santuario Yasaka]] [[fa:معبد یاساکا]] [[fr:Yasaka-jinja]] [[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]] [[id:Kuil Yasaka]] [[it:Santuario di Yasaka]] [[ja:八坂神社]] [[ko:야사카 신사]] [[pt:Santuário Yasaka]] [[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]] [[simple:Yasaka Shrine]] [[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]] [[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]] [[uk:Храм Ясака]] [[zh:八坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q692714}} [[Category:656 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 650s]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. [[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ]]{{Gion cult}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[en:Yasaka Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]] {{Commons category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[de:Yasaka-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]] [[es:Santuario Yasaka]] [[fa:معبد یاساکا]] [[fr:Yasaka-jinja]] [[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]] [[id:Kuil Yasaka]] [[it:Santuario di Yasaka]] [[ja:八坂神社]] [[ko:야사카 신사]] [[pt:Santuário Yasaka]] [[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]] [[simple:Yasaka Shrine]] [[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]] [[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]] [[uk:Храм Ясака]] [[zh:八坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q692714}} [[Category:656 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:pages with conflicting qids]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 650s]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]] [[Category:pages]]

[[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gion District, Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}} | image = Yasaka jinja.jpg | caption = Yasaka Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|13|N|135|46|43|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]{{efn|Originally [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]]}}
[[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine|1=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen|1=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park|1=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami|1=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. revision.text:
[[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gion District, Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}} | image = Yasaka jinja.jpg | caption = Yasaka Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|13|N|135|46|43|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]{{efn|Originally [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]]}}
[[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine|1=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen|1=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park|1=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami|1=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ]]{{Gion cult}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[en:Yasaka Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]] {{Commons category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[de:Yasaka-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]] [[es:Santuario Yasaka]] [[fa:معبد یاساکا]] [[fr:Yasaka-jinja]] [[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]] [[id:Kuil Yasaka]] [[it:Santuario di Yasaka]] [[ja:八坂神社]] [[ko:야사카 신사]] [[pt:Santuário Yasaka]] [[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]] [[simple:Yasaka Shrine]] [[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]] [[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]] [[uk:Храм Ясака]] [[zh:八坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q692714}} [[Category:656 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 650s]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]] [[Category:Pages with unclosed square brackets]] [[Category:Pages without translation templates]]

[https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[Category:sn_crud|TESTcommons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gion District, Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}} | image = Yasaka jinja.jpg | caption = Yasaka Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|13|N|135|46|43|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]{{efn|Originally [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]]}}
[[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine|1=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen|1=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park|1=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami|1=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ]]{{Gion cult}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[en:Yasaka Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]] {{Commons category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[de:Yasaka-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]] [[es:Santuario Yasaka]] [[fa:معبد یاساکا]] [[fr:Yasaka-jinja]] [[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]] [[id:Kuil Yasaka]] [[it:Santuario di Yasaka]] [[ja:八坂神社]] [[ko:야사카 신사]] [[pt:Santuário Yasaka]] [[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]] [[simple:Yasaka Shrine]] [[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]] [[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]] [[uk:Храм Ясака]] [[zh:八坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q692714}} [[Category:656 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Pages with simple english wikipedia interwikis]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 650s]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]

(2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[co:commons:Category:Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] [[bcl:Yasaka Shrine]][[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]][[de:Yasaka-Schrein]][[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]][[en:Yasaka Shrine]][[es:Santuario Yasaka]][[fa:معبد یاساکا]][[fr:Yasaka-jinja]][[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]][[id:Kuil Yasaka]][[it:Santuario di Yasaka]][[ja:八坂神社]][[ko:야사카 신사]][[my:ယဆခနတ်ကျောင်း]][[pl:Yasaka-jinja]][[pt:Santuário Yasaka]][[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]][[simple:Yasaka Shrine]][[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]][[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]][[uk:Храм Ясака]][[zh:八坂神社]] {{Short description|Shinto shrine in Gion District, Kyoto, Japan}} {{Infobox religious building | name = {{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}} | image = Yasaka jinja.jpg | caption = Yasaka Shrine | map_type = Japan | map_alt = | coordinates = {{coord|35|00|13|N|135|46|43|E|region:JP_type:landmark_source:dewiki|display=title,inline}} | map_relief = | map_size = | map_caption = | religious_affiliation = [[Shinto]] | type = | deity = [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto]]{{efn|Originally [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]]}}
[[Yashimajinumi]] | founded_by = | established = 656 | date_destroyed = | location = [[Gion]], {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}} | website = | architecture_style = {{ill|Gion zukuri|ja|祇園造|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/Gion zukuri|1=Gion-zukuri|comment=changed title based on wikidata|WD=Q11588450}} | festival = | leadership = }} '''{{nihongo|Yasaka Shrine|八坂神社|Yasaka-jinja}}''', once called '''{{nihongo|Gion Shrine|祇園神社|''Gion-jinja''}}''', is a [[Shinto]] shrine in the [[Gion]] District of {{ill|Kyoto|en|Kyoto|WD=Q34600|lt=Kyoto|1=Kyoto}}, {{ill|Japan|en|Japan|WD=Q17|lt=Japan|1=Japan}}. Situated at the east end of Shijō-dōri (Fourth Avenue), the shrine includes several buildings, including gates, a main hall and a stage. The Yasaka shrine is dedicated to [[Susanoo-no-Mikoto|Susanoo]] in the tradition of the [[Gion Faith|Gion faith]] as its chief kami, with his consort [[Kushinadahime]] on the east, and eight offspring deities (yahashira no mikogami) on the west. The yahashira no mikogami include Yashimajinumi no kami, Itakeru no kami, Ōyatsuhime no kami, Tsumatsuhime no kami, Ōtoshi no kami, Ukanomitama no kami, Ōyatsuhiko no kami, and Suseribime no mikoto.{{Cite web |date=21 April 2005 |title=Mikogami |url=http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |access-date=29 September 2019 |website=Encyclopedia of Shinto|archive-url=https://web.archive.org/web/20070930212535/http://eos.kokugakuin.ac.jp/modules/xwords/entry.php?entryID=13 |archive-date=2007-09-30 }} ==History== Initial construction on the Shrine began in 656. The Shrine became the object of Imperial patronage during the early {{ill|Heian period|en|Heian period|WD=Q193292|lt=Heian period|1=Heian period}}.Breen, John ''et al.'' (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=oharano ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami,'' pp. 74-75.] According to the [[Engishiki]], the much lesser known {{ill|Nunakuma Shrine|en|Nunakuma Shrine|WD=Q11554446|lt=Nunakuma Shrine|1=Nunakuma Shrine}} in {{ill|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|en|Fukuyama, Hiroshima|WD=Q661038|lt=Fukuyama|1=Fukuyama, Hiroshima}} is the source by which [[Gozu Tennō|Gozu Tenno]] entered [[Yasaka Shrine]] through [[:en:Kanjo|Kanjo]].{{Cite journal |last=Faure |first=Bernard |date=2021-01-01 |title=GODS OF MEDIEVAL JAPAN - Vol. 3 |url=https://www.academia.edu/60480771/GODS_OF_MEDIEVAL_JAPAN_Vol_3_Rage_and_Ravage |journal=Rage and Ravage}}{{Rp|page=124}} In 965, {{ill|Emperor Murakami|en|Emperor Murakami|WD=Q374015|lt=Emperor Murakami|1=Emperor Murakami}} ordered that Imperial messengers be sent to report important events to the guardian ''kami'' of Japan. These ''heihaku'' were initially presented to 16 shrines;Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1962). ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines,'' pp. 116-117. and in 991, {{ill|Emperor Ichijō|en|Emperor Ichijō|WD=Q449540|lt=Emperor Ichijō|1=Emperor Ichijō}} added three more shrines to Murakami's list. Three years later in 994, Ichijō refined the scope of that composite list by adding [[Umenomiya Taisha|Umenomiya Shrine]] and Gion Shrine.Ponsonby-Fane, ''Shrines,'' p. 118. From 1871 through 1946, Yasaka Shrine was officially designated one of the {{nihongo|''[[Modern system of Ranked Shinto shrines|Kanpei-taisha]]''|官幣大社}}, meaning that it stood in the first Rank of government supported shrines.Ponsonby-Fane, Richard. (1959). ''The Imperial House of Japan,'' pp. 126. ==Matsuri== In the year 869, a terrible epidemic caused the Emperor to call for a Shinto ritual to appease vengeful spirits believed to be the cause. Representatives used 66 pikes (''hoko'' or 鉾) for each of 66 regions in the country at that time, in a Shinto ritual called ''goryo-e'' (御霊会) at ''{{ill|Shinsenen|en|Shinsenen|WD=Q11590468|lt=Shinsenen|1=Shinsenen}}'', a lake at the Imperial Palace at that time. Eventually, the ritual became an annual event known as the Gion Goryo-e, and then the [[Gion Matsuri]], associated with Gion Shrine, in the Gion district of Kyoto. The pikes became decorated, larger, and eventually morphed into the famous Gion Matsuri ''yamaboko'' (山鉾) floats.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} These travel through the central streets of Kyoto, as do ''[[mikoshi]]'' (portable shrines) from Yasaka Shrine, to purify the streets and ward off any potential epidemics or other harm. The Gion Matsuri takes place every July. Today, in addition to hosting the Gion Matsuri, Yasaka Shrine welcomes thousands of people every New Year, for traditional [[Japanese New Year]] rituals and celebrations. In April, the crowds pass through the temple on their way to {{ill|Maruyama Park|en|Maruyama Park|WD=Q1108695|lt=Maruyama Park|1=Maruyama Park}}, a popular ''{{ill|Hanami|en|Hanami|WD=Q210150|lt=Hanami|1=Hanami}}'' (cherry blossom viewing) site. Lanterns decorate the stage with the names of festival sponsors. ==Gallery== image:Yasaka Shrine 01.jpg|Main gate of the Yasaka shrine. image:Yasaka_Shrine_Kyoto.png|Stage at night during the annual Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine1.jpg|Crowds during the Gion Matsuri. image:YasakaShrine2.jpg|A statue outside of the shrine. image:YasakaShrine3.jpg|The mikoshi of the Yasaka shrine, displayed for part of the Gion Matsuri in the second week of July.{{cite book |last1=Pawasarat |first1=Catherine |title=The Gion Festival: Exploring Its Mysteries |date=July 2020 |publisher=Independent |isbn=978-0-9985886-6-7 |page=28 |edition=1st |url=https://www.gionfestival.org/book |access-date=2 December 2022}} == See also== * {{ill|List of Shinto shrines|en|List of Shinto shrines|WD=Q120679573|lt=List of Shinto shrines|1=List of Shinto shrines}} * {{ill|Twenty-Two Shrines|en|Twenty-Two Shrines|WD=Q215320|lt=Twenty-Two Shrines|1=Twenty-Two Shrines}} * [[Modern system of Ranked Shinto Shrines]] ==Notes== {{notelist}} {{reflist}} ==References== {{commons Category:short pages]][[Category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} * [[John Breen (scholar)|Breen, John]] and [[Mark Teeuwen]]. (2000). [https://books.google.com/books?id=MADlfH002mAC&q=nijunisha ''Shinto in History: Ways of the Kami.''] Honolulu: [[:en:University of Hawaii Press|University of Hawaii Press]]. {{ISBN|978-0-8248-2363-4}} * {{ill|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|en|Richard Ponsonby-Fane|WD=Q3246771|lt=Ponsonby-Fane, Richard|1=Richard Ponsonby-Fane}}. (1962). [https://books.google.com/books?id=tjEvAAAAYAAJ&q=Studies+in+Shinto+and+Shrines ''Studies in Shinto and Shrines.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/3994492 OCLC 399449] * ____________. (1959). [https://books.google.com/books?id=SLAeAAAAMAAJ&q=The+Imperial+House+of+Japan ''The Imperial House of Japan.''] Kyoto: Ponsonby Memorial Society. [http://www.worldcat.org/wcpa/oclc/194887 OCLC 194887] ]]{{Gion cult}} {{Beppyo Shrines}} \ {{Iwanaga Faith}} [[en:Yasaka Shrine]] [[ca:Santuari de Yasaka]] {{Commons category|Yasaka-jinja (Kyoto)}} [[de:Yasaka-Schrein]] [[el:Ιερό Γιασάκα]] [[es:Santuario Yasaka]] [[fa:معبد یاساکا]] [[fr:Yasaka-jinja]] [[hu:Jaszaka-szentély]] [[id:Kuil Yasaka]] [[it:Santuario di Yasaka]] [[ja:八坂神社]] [[ko:야사카 신사]] [[pt:Santuário Yasaka]] [[ru:Ясака дзиндзя]] [[simple:Yasaka Shrine]] [[th:ศาลเจ้ายาซากะ]] [[tr:Yasaka Tapınağı]] [[uk:Храм Ясака]] [[zh:八坂神社]] {{wikidata link|Q692714}} [[Category:656 establishments]] [[Category:7th-century establishments in Japan]] [[Category:Beppyo shrines]] [[Category:Gion shrines]] [[Category:Important Cultural Properties of Japan]] [[Category:Kanpei Taisha]] [[Category:Kokushi genzaisha]] [[Category:Religious buildings and structures completed in the 650s]] [[Category:Shinbutsu bunri]] [[Category:Shinto shrines in Kyoto]] [[Category:Twenty-Two Shrines]] [[Category:Wikidata has short description]]
